sections.content:
Types of Numbers

Numbers come in different families. Natural numbers (1, 2, 3, …) are what you use to count. Integers include negatives too (…, −2, −1, 0, 1, 2, …). Rational numbers can be written as a fraction — ½, −3, 0.75 are all rational. Irrational numbers like √2 and π cannot be written as exact fractions; their decimals go on forever without repeating.

Understanding which type of number you're working with matters — it determines what operations are valid and what form your answer should be in.
🔑 Key words: Factor — a number that divides exactly into another. Multiple — the result of multiplying a number by an integer. Prime — a number with exactly two factors: 1 and itself.

Factors, Multiples & Prime Numbers

The factors of 12 are: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12 — every number that divides into 12 exactly. The multiples of 4 are 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, … — the 4 times table going on forever.

Prime numbers (2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, …) are the atoms of arithmetic — every other whole number can be broken down into primes. The process is called prime factorisation: 60 = 2 × 2 × 3 × 5 = 2² × 3 × 5. This is useful for finding HCF and LCM.

HCF and LCM

The Highest Common Factor (HCF) of two numbers is the largest factor they share. To find it, write out the prime factorisation of each and multiply the common prime factors together.

Example: HCF(24, 36). 24 = 2³ × 3, 36 = 2² × 3². Common factors: 2² × 3 = 12. So HCF = 12.

The Lowest Common Multiple (LCM) is the smallest number both divide into. Take each prime factor at its highest power: LCM(24, 36) = 2³ × 3² = 72.

A quick check: HCF × LCM = product of the two numbers (24 × 36 = 864 = 12 × 72 ✓).

Index (Power) Notation

Instead of writing 2 × 2 × 2 × 2, we write 2⁴ (read: '2 to the power 4'). The base is 2, the index (exponent) is 4. Key rules:

  • Multiplying same base: aᵐ × aⁿ = aᵐ⁺ⁿ
  • Dividing same base: aᵐ ÷ aⁿ = aᵐ⁻ⁿ
  • Power of a power: (aᵐ)ⁿ = aᵐⁿ
  • Zero power: a⁰ = 1 (for any non-zero a)

These laws will come up constantly in algebra, so practise them until they're automatic.

questions.question: Which of the following is a prime number?
questions.explanation: 17 has exactly two factors: 1 and 17. It cannot be divided evenly by any other whole number. 9 = 3², 15 = 3×5, 21 = 3×7 — all composite.

questions.question: What is the HCF of 18 and 24?
Which of these is an irrational number?
questions.explanation: √5 ≈ 2.2360679… — it never terminates or repeats, so it is irrational. 0.333… = ⅓ (rational), ¾ is rational, −7 is an integer (rational).
